regulator: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)
applicable_law: SEBI (Issue of Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2018 (ICDR Regulations)
definition: An IPO is when a private company offers its shares to the public for the first time to raise capital and get listed on a stock exchange (BSE/NSE).
simple_analogy: A company is like a large cake. In an IPO, the owners sell some slices of that cake to the public. When you buy an IPO, you become a part-owner (shareholder) of that company.

types.name: Book Building IPO
types.description: Price is discovered through bidding in a price band (e.g. ₹100-110). Investors bid at any price within the band. Final issue price is determined by demand. Most common type.
types.price_band: Upper limit cannot exceed 120% of lower limit

types.name: Fixed Price IPO
types.description: Price is fixed in advance. Investors apply at the announced price. Less common.

types.name: SME IPO
types.description: IPO by Small and Medium Enterprises listed on BSE SME or NSE Emerge platform. Smaller companies, higher risk.

key_terms.Price Band: The range within which investors can bid. E.g. ₹200-210 means you can bid at any price between ₹200 and ₹210.
key_terms.Cut-off Price: You bid at 'cut-off' to indicate you accept whatever final price SEBI determines. Recommended for retail investors.
key_terms.Lot Size: Minimum number of shares you must apply for. E.g. lot size of 50 means minimum application = 50 shares × issue price.
key_terms.Subscription: How many times an IPO is applied for vs shares available. '5x subscribed' means 5 times more demand than supply.
key_terms.Oversubscription: When IPO receives more applications than shares available. Allotment is done by lottery for retail investors.
key_terms.GMP (Grey Market Premium): Unofficial market price of IPO shares before listing. NOT regulated by SEBI. Purely indicative and speculative.
key_terms.Anchor Investor: Institutional investor allocated shares before IPO opens. Positive anchor allocation signals confidence in the issue.
key_terms.DRHP: Draft Red Herring Prospectus — the detailed IPO document filed with SEBI. Contains all material information about the company.
key_terms.RHP: Red Herring Prospectus — final prospectus with issue price/price band filled in.
key_terms.Listing Date: The day shares start trading on the stock exchange after IPO allotment.
key_terms.Listing Gains: Profit if share price on listing day is higher than issue price. Not guaranteed.
key_terms.Basis of Allotment: Document published after IPO closure showing how shares were distributed across applicants.
key_terms.ASBA: Application Supported by Blocked Amount — your bank account is blocked (not debited) until allotment. If not allotted, money is unblocked automatically.
key_terms.OFS: Offer for Sale — existing shareholders selling their shares in the IPO. Company does NOT receive this money; it goes to selling shareholders.
key_terms.Fresh Issue: Company issuing new shares to raise fresh capital for the business.
key_terms.IPO Proceeds Use: Must be disclosed in DRHP — typically for capex, debt repayment, working capital, acquisitions.

investor_categories.reservation: Minimum 35% of IPO shares reserved for retail investors
investor_categories.allotment: Lottery-based if oversubscribed — every applicant has equal chance of getting 1 lot
investor_categories.tip: Apply at cut-off price for best chance of allotment

investor_categories.category: NII / HNI (Non-Institutional Investor / High Net Worth Individual)

sub_categories.sNII: ₹2 lakh to ₹10 lakh — 1/3rd of NII portion reserved
sub_categories.bNII: Above ₹10 lakh — 2/3rd of NII portion reserved

investor_categories.eligibility: Mutual funds, FIIs, banks, insurance companies, etc.
Employees of the company going public
investor_categories.benefit: Often offered at a discount to issue price

investor_categories.eligibility: Existing shareholders of the promoter group / parent company

how_to_apply: Step 1: Ensure you have a Demat account + trading account + bank account (all three linked)
Step 2: Complete KYC if not done
Step 3: Read the RHP to understand the company's business, financials, and risk factors
Step 4: Log into your broker's app or bank's net banking (ASBA)
Step 5: Search for the IPO, enter lot quantity and price (choose cut-off for retail)
Step 6: Funds are blocked in your bank account via ASBA
Step 7: Wait for allotment (typically 6 days after IPO closes)
Step 8: Check allotment status on BSE/NSE website or registrar's website
Step 9: If allotted, shares credited to Demat account on T+1 before listing; refunds processed for unallotted applications
Step 10: Shares start trading on listing day
sebi_investor_protections: Mandatory 6-day timeline from IPO close to listing
ASBA ensures money is not debited until allotment — no risk of fund misuse
25% of promoter shares locked in for 18 months post-IPO
Anchor investors locked in for 30-90 days
SEBI reviews DRHP and issues observations before IPO can open
Price band upper limit restricted to 120% of lower limit for book building
Refund within 15 days for unallotted applications (interest payable if delayed)
risks: Listing price can be below issue price — listing loss possible
GMP is speculative — do not base investment decisions on GMP
Overvalued IPOs may underperform in the long run even if they list at a premium
Company may not use IPO proceeds as stated
SME IPOs are higher risk due to smaller, less-established businesses
Lock-in periods mean promoters cannot sell immediately, but this does not guarantee price stability
disclaimer: IPO investments are subject to market risks. Past listing performance is not indicative of future returns. Read the Red Herring Prospectus carefully before applying.
